channel.title: Posts on Journey of Machine Learning
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Journey of Machine Learning

item.title: Adversarial Examples
item.description: As deep learning (DL) being applied in many safety-critical environments such as self-driving cars, the reliability and rebustness of DL models are highly desired. However, the recent emergence of adversarial examples posed doubts of our confidence in even state of the art DL models. In this post, let’s see some methods of creating and defensing adversarial examples. An adversarial example is a sample of input data which has been modified very slightly in a way that the modification are imperceptible to human but can easily fool deep neural networks.

item.title: Trainging generative models as divergence minimization
item.description: Generative models can be used for data compression, denoising, inpainting, image synthesis, semi-supervised learning, unsupervised representation learning, and other tasks. There are many kinds of generative models, and among which GAN, VAE and autoregressive models are the most popular ones. A great blog on Generative Models written by OpenAI is worth reading at length. In this blog, we mainly discuss generative models from a divergence minimization perspective. Generative models, as its name explains, it can be used to generate data, the prerequisite is to understand the training dataset, i.

item.title: Generative Adversarial Networks
item.description: Generative Adversarial Networks(GAN) [1] has become one of the most powerful techniques in machine learning since its emergence in 2014. The interesting idea of training methods and the flexible design of the objective function make GAN have numerous variants, and these variants even consist of a GAN zoo. Let’s first interpret GAN from perspectives and then see how to implement GAN in Tensorflow: Game Theory GAN consists of two models: a generative model $G$ that tries to generate realistic samples, and a discriminative model $D$ that tries to classify data from both $G$ and dataset.

item.title: Variational Autoencoder
item.description: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) can be seen as a stochastic version of conventional autoencoder which imposes some constraints on the distribution of latent variables, however, the original VAE was derived from Variational Bayesian method and linked to Autoencoder just because some terms in objective function can be interpreted as an encoder and a decoder. Let’s first derive VAE from scratch and then we see how to implement it in Tensorflow.
